item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poor Researcher James… it was in his mind all along…

Anyway. The article specified that this thing preys on human beings but it didn't seem to specify how, exactly. It lays eggs in the brain but doesn't seem to gain any sustenance in the process (it's very possible I missed it entirely)? Also, is the entire species female or do the males lay eggs as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. The trope of the life you're living not being real is a bit overdone, but this still made me feel uncomfortable.

Also, I'm pretty sure that based on its properties, this thing should be a "Flashforward bug". :P

item.title: Re: It's line-by-line time
item.content:encoded:
if you compared your human DNA to your mom's DNA

I dunno why, but this just sounds funny to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! It's a pretty well written article in my opinion, but I would like to see the DNA copying aspect of the SCP explored a little more in depth. After all, being able to get someone's DNA from something as detaches as someone's memory of the person is a pretty big deal!

It's a nice twist, and I understand your inclination towards putting it at the very end of the article, but I think it would benefit more if it was expanded. If you still want to keep it as a twist, maybe put it as an update to a previous version of the (in universe) article? Like MaliceAforethought pointed out, that's not really how DNA works, but you can easily fix that by expanding onto the idea.

There's a lot of questions one could ask about this aspect of the SCP. What is the DNA being used for? Where is the SCP's "actual" DNA being stored, if it has any? Does the DNA of different kind of people have an effect on the growth of the SCP? Just to name a few.

Overall, great concept and a +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this piece. It does a very good job at conveying the fear that our entire lives could just be dreams (maybe it's just me, I dunno). The bug thing is a nice amount of ick, but not so much that it starts to distract the reader from the piece.

+1

item.title: Re: It's line-by-line time
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the line-by-line. I've always struggled with these kinds of horror pieces, and I appreciate having someone go through it.

+ Response
- Response

I went through and fixed the problems you pointed out. As for the fact that it's a spider… I decided to make it a millipede. Imagining having all of those legs crawl over your brain. (In addition, they have a much simpler diet, which takes care of those bits :-) )

I'm not sure what to do about the interview; it's hard to write a convincing 10-year-old. I feel like anything else would feel unnecessarily expository.

For the DNA stuff, if you compared your human DNA to your mom's DNA, it would be a 50% match. Yes, the rest of it would be a 99.9% percent match, but the part that we actually care about would be 50%. This is what is actually happening here.

item.title: It's line-by-line time
item.content:encoded:
I love this conceptually, but the more I think about it the more things stand out at me and stop me from upvoting.

+ Show critique
- Hide this

SCP-4872 is to be fed a diet of small flies and other typical arachnid prey.

I'm fairly sure the prey of arachnids vary wildly by species. This reads like somebody didn't want to look up what this spider actually ate. At the very least, make something up — if someone more knowledgeable corrects you on it, then they've done the research for you anyway!

The population of this chamber is never to exceed 50.

I get that this is implied to be "fifty SCP-4872 specimens", but it's never specified. What if there's a live food-bug in there — do they have to kill a spider to make room for it?

Interaction with SCP-4872 is not to take place without wearing a padded suit at least six centimeters thick and without any opening greater than one centimeter in size.

The way you've ordered the subjects of this sentence mean "Don't interact without two things. 1) This special padded suit. 2) An opening greater than one centimetre in size". I'm fairly sure that's the exact opposite of what you wanted to say.

Biweekly inspection of SCP-4872's containment cell is to take place

A minor thing, feel free to disregard, but "SCP-4872's containment cell is to be inspected biweekly" seems like the far more natural way of phrasing this.

Although SCP-4872 are capable of sustaining themselves from small bugs, their preferred prey are humans.

This is a good line. Not as "oh shit" as some other articles, but it sets up the stakes well pretty early in the text. I do think it should be "sustaining themselves on small bugs", however.

the venom of SCP-4872 has anesthesiac properties

The word you're looking for is either "anaesthetic", "analgesic", or just "numbing". An anaesthesiac would really just be… somebody with anaesthesia.

which can cause numbness at the point of injection

Another good line. Made me physically uncomfortable as I realised what these spiders were doing to people.

such as schools

I'd personally give a couple more examples, with schools as the rule-of-three stinger. That's just me, though.

This will cause the subject to experience a "continued" version of their life while asleep.

This gives the game away far too early.

the larvae will leave its host through the host's ears

Gross, but why not the nose, mouth, and eyes as well? Or the incision they entered through?

Brain activity is believed to be used as a form of sustenance by SCP-4872 and its larvae

This is some wild speculation from Researchers. The reader can draw the conclusion from the second half of the sentence anyway.

Addendum 4872-1: Affected Subject Interview

This whole section was by far the worst-written part of the article. It felt stilted, flat, impersonal, and overly contrived to give a pretty naff expository dump on an otherwise solid piece. I dunno what I'd do to convey the same information, but it wouldn't be poor little Jimmy's first kiss.

For instance, a 50% match to an Alabama seamstress, a 40% match to a Philadelphia criminal case lawyer, a 43% match to a Philadelphia dog trainer, and a 12% match to the current mayor of Philadelphia.

This is a good detail, but — and I'm not a biologist — I'm 99% sure that's not how DNA works. I think you can get matches with x% certainty, and I think that would be a reasonable thing to put here? But I'm also reasonably certain that any differences between people's DNA, given that they're the same species, would be around 0.1%. Like, I think we share about half of our genes with bananas, and that seamstress doesn't strike me as a fruit. This is, of course, based on half-remembered biology lessons and wikipedia, so please don't crucify me if I'm wrong; it just struck me as contrary to how I thought things worked.

And, finally, one more note — I really wish these weren't spiders. I get that they're a good fit, and people are already scared of them which makes for an initial boost to the horror factor — they're just so overused. There are a lot more interesting creepy-crawlies out there, and most of 'em haven't had SCP articles yet. Spiders, on the other hand, already have 32 articles, and they lessen the memorability of any specific scip substantially in my eyes.

Most of this is subjective, of course, and even more of is non-fact-checked, but there's enough problems that I feel this could use a tune-up regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spider that lays eggs in your brain and eats memories while you dream a better version of your own life.

I hate it.

Take your +1.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Take 2 of this one. Hopefully the ending should work now.

Thank you to Popsioak, JakdragonX, Lambda Fairy, Captain Kirby, Alces_alces, Almarduk, and JackalRelated for critique! Image comes from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Millipede_August_2009-1.jpg
